title: Interference of local-measurement histories
authors: Parveen Kumar, Igor V. Gornyi, Yuval Gefen
abstract: The evolution of a quantum system comprises two fundamental processes--continuous unitary dynamics and stochastic measurement-induced jumps. The latter are often viewed as a source of decoherence. Can two histories of such an evolution, made up of local measurements, interfere with each other? Here, we answer this question in the affirmative. A manifestation of this interference is the generation of entanglement between two parts of the system that are individually coupled to distinct detectors. Specifically, we develop a protocol in which two alternative local measurement processes act on a pair of qubits, and show how interference of histories is generated under coherent control, leading to entanglement. Furthermore, we find that averaging over the detectors' readouts still results in an entangled (albeit not maximally entangled) state. Our results extend the notion of quantum interference beyond unitary evolution to genuinely measurement-driven dynamics, and identify limits on the generation of quantum correlations using interference of measurement histories.
summary: The paper shows that interference between different sequences of local measurements can create entanglement between two qubits. While the entanglement is limited when measurement outcomes are averaged, the protocol can still produce Bell nonlocality, providing a new way to generate quantum correlations.
main_problem: Investigating whether two different histories of quantum evolution, composed of local measurements, can interfere with each other to generate quantum correlations.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that interference between measurement-induced histories can generate entanglement and Bell nonlocality, even when the individual measurement branches are non-entangling.
method: The study uses Kraus operators to describe measurement-induced evolution and employs a protocol involving an auxiliary control qubit to place measurement branches in a coherent superposition.
model_or_system: A bipartite system of two qubits (A and B) initially in a separable state, subject to local generalized measurements controlled by an auxiliary qubit (C).
key_observables: Concurrence (to measure entanglement) and the CHSH parameter/Horodecki criterion (to measure Bell nonlocality).
important_parameters: Measurement strength (g), measurement-axis rotation angle (theta), and the postselection of the control qubit state.
main_assumptions: The proof of the impossibility of maximal entanglement after averaging assumes that the resulting averaged state must be pure to reach maximal entanglement.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of the protocol, illustrating how a control qubit selects between two measurement branches on qubits A and B.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of measurement-history interference, presents a protocol using a control qubit, analyzes entanglement generation in postselected and averaged regimes, provides a microscopic Hamiltonian derivation, and concludes with a mathematical proof regarding the limits of entanglement.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for open quantum systems and quantum optics as it identifies a new mechanism for generating entanglement through non-unitary, measurement-driven dynamics, extending the concept of quantum interference beyond unitary evolution.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Coherently controlled measurement histories. The control qubit C selects between two alternative branches of local measurements acting on system qubits A and B. The left (right) branch corresponds to the measurement-induced history conditioned on |0⟩C (|1⟩C). The respective solid and wiggly interaction lines denote that the local measurement Hamiltonians in the two branches may differ, corresponding to distinct measurement observables. After postselection of the control, amplitudes associated with the two histories interfere. This interference occurs within each fixed detector-readout sector, while different readout sectors remain orthogonal and combine incoherently.

Figure 2. Concurrence Cavg(g, θ) [cf. Eq. (16)] of the readout- averaged state for the two-qubit example, as a function of measurement strength g and angle θ. The solid white curve denotes the CHSH Bell-nonlocality boundary γ(g, θ) = 1, ob- tained from the Horodecki criterion for two-qubit states [58]. The figure shows two distinct regimes: an entangled but Bell- local region (γ ≤1) and a Bell-nonlocal region (γ > 1), cf. Appendix D. Although entanglement is generated over a broad parameter range, the concurrence remains strictly below unity for all g and θ, consistent with the constraint im- posed by readout averaging.
